title: Chapter 23: The Silence of Green Leaves
paragraphs: When darkness dissipates, leaving behind only ash, time loses its meaning. The Great Battle at the Gate of Lamentation ended not with a victory cry, but with an absolute, ringing void. The Behemoth of the Void crumbled into gray dust, unable to withstand its own power, but none of the survivors looked at the fallen enemy.
All eyes were fixed on the edge of the smoking crater.

Avotu, the last apprentice of Sivarus, an elf whose name for centuries inspired terror in enemies and awe in allies, slowly rose to his feet. His armor was covered in dents, his breath came from his chest with a whistle, but the physical pain was insignificant compared to what was currently happening in his mind.
He walked with a staggering gait to the edge of the crater. There, on the charred, acid-scorched stone, lay what remained of Yashil.

The little body of the goblin was hopelessly broken. His fragile bones could not withstand even the residual shockwave of concentrated entropy. His chest was unnaturally crushed, and from under the torn burlap, thick green blood pulsed slowly, soaking the gray ash.

Avotu sank heavily to his knees. A great warrior who for millennia believed that his duty was to carry the grief of the entire world on his shoulders, looked at a being that this world considered filth. A goblin, possessing no magic, knowing no art of the sword, had just pushed him from death. He had given his only, tiny life for one who himself sought destruction.

The black flame on Avotu's head flickered. It no longer devoured the light around it. In its unsearchable depths, where guilt and despair had accumulated for centuries, golden sparks flared up. They did not burn—they warmed. Avotu understood that this sacrifice was not a reproach. It was a gift.
From behind came a sound that made blood freeze. It was not the roar of a beast or the moan of a wounded man. It was the sound of a breaking soul.

Kirah, the giant demon of destruction, crawlingly, dragging his torn paws behind him, made his way through the jade wreckage. He stopped beside Yashil. The huge resinous creature was afraid to even touch the broken body of the goblin, so as not to cause him even more pain. From the burning ruby eyes of the demon, hissing and evaporating on the cold stones, rolled huge, impossible tears.

"Master..." Kirah rasped. His voice shook so much that the words could barely be made out. "Why... why did you step forward? You were supposed to hide behind my back. I was created for this. Not you."

Rene approached next. The fox moved slowly, as if each step cost her unbearable effort. Her perfect posture was broken. Her yellow eyes, which always looked at the world with cynical boredom, were now wide open and full of tears that smeared the cherry paint on her lips. She dropped to her knees right into the mud, not caring about her torn stockings, and carefully, with her fingertips, touched Yashil's cooling green hand.
"You are a disgusting egoist, Green Philosopher," Rene whispered, and her voice broke into a sob. "You made us believe that there is meaning in this rotting world... and you leave yourself."

Dzhanavar silently approached the crater. The werewolf did not utter any quotes. In his wolf muzzle, there was not a trace of his former lazy mockery. The huge predator drove his double-bladed axe deep into the stone at Yashil's head, as if erecting a tombstone, and heavily bowed his head, closing his eyes. The wolf mourned his pack leader.

Lord Valtor stood a distance away. The vampire looked at this scene, and in his blood-colored eyes there was not the usual centuries-old indifference. He waved a hand, and the legion of undead that had saved the refugees silently crumbled into dust, returning to the earth. The immortal lord slowly, with absolute reverence, bowed his head to the mortal goblin.
Yashil opened his eyes with difficulty. His breathing was shallow, with a light, wet bubbling. He tried to focus his gaze on the faces of those leaning over him.
The goblin's lips flickered faintly, barely noticeably, in an attempt to smile.

"Old Gnawer..." Yashil's voice was quieter than the rustle of falling ash, but in the ringing silence each sound echoed like thunder. "Old Gnawer used to say... giving is the lot of fools. In this world... only the one who takes survives."
The goblin convulsively inhaled, green blood bubbling on his lips. Avotu carefully slid a plate gauntlet under his head, lifting it.

"My mother..." Yashil continued, looking through the leaden clouds of Agdash as if he saw beyond them the clean, clear stars of a distant past. "My mother gave everything. She wasn't a fool, Kirah. She was... the strongest."
He turned his fading gaze to the huge demon.
"Don't let your darkness... decide who you are, my friend. You are a good one, Kirah. Warm those who are cold."
Then Yashil looked at Rene and Dzhanavar.
"Protect each other. You are... the best pack."
Finally, his dark, wise eyes met the bottomless pits of Avotu's eyes.

"You promised to be a shield, traveler," the goblin whispered. "Your pain... is just your love that has nowhere to go. Give it to those who are still alive. And then... no one will vanish into the darkness forever."
With a heavy, grinding sound, the small valve of Agdash's Great Gates opened slightly.

From there, stumbling and dropping weapons, came elven guards. They were led by the same captain who had once struck Yashil in the face. They stood on the balcony. They had seen everything. They had seen how the High Elves closed the doors in the face of death, and how a tiny goblin stepped forward to meet it.

The captain slowly approached the edge of the crater. His golden armor, symbol of centuries-old superiority, seemed to him now a heavy, suffocating cage. The elf looked at the green blood mixed with ash. He looked at the demon mourning the goblin, and at the great Black Flame holding him in his arms.
The centuries-old wall of elven pride, racial hatred, and blind arrogance collapsed.

The captain unbuckled his baldric. His richly decorated sword fell to the stones with a clang. The elf slowly dropped to his knees before Yashil. After him, one by one, tossing aside helmets and dropping halberds, all the guards of Agdash knelt. The crowd of saved refugees—gnomes, hydrols, half-orks—followed their example.

Hundreds of creatures of different races bowed their heads in absolute, reverent silence. Not before magic. Not before the power of ancient lords. But before the greatness of the spirit of the little goblin from the Rotten Hollow.

Yashil saw this. His eyes filled with quiet, all-encompassing, boundless peace. He had dreamed all his life of proving that his people were not monsters. That kindness could pierce the armor of any hatred.
"Mama..." Yashil exhaled, barely audible. "Look... they are not afraid of us anymore."
The light in the goblin's dark eyes went out softly, without torment. His chest lowered slowly for the last time and did not rise again.
The wind over the wastelands died down completely.

Avotu carefully, with infinite tenderness, lowered Yashil's head onto the stone. The black flame on the elf's head flared with a steady, warming golden light. He took the goblin's words into the very depth of his tormented heart. Now he had a goal. He would be a shield.

In a fading world where each day was saturated with the egoism of survival, one single life had overturned everything. And though Yashil of the Rotten Hollow had closed his eyes forever, his light could no longer be extinguished.
